text: Precise control over dimensions and spatially defined functionality remains a central challenge in nanoparticle engineering. Living crystallization-driven self-assembly (CDSA) has recently gained prominence as a robust strategy for producing one-dimensional and two-dimensional core–shell micellar architectures via the seeded growth of polymeric and molecular amphiphiles. However, a major bottleneck remains: epitaxial growth is typically restricted to a single core chemistry. This limitation hinders the fabrication of complex nanoparticles featuring segmented cores with diverse compositions—architectures that are critical for achieving selective chemical transformations or specialized responses to external stimuli. In this study, we demonstrate a seeded growth approach utilizing various crystallizable polylactone homopolymer and block copolymer blend combinations to produce 2D platelet micelles with compositionally distinct, segmented cores. To highlight the practical utility of this structural control, we show that the internal core chemistry can be exploited to achieve spatially selective hydrolytic degradation. These findings offer a blueprint for designing complex, stimuli-responsive particles tailored for programmed-release and advanced cargo-delivery applications.
